textPlain: this is great news and I wonder if and how this has impact on other European deployments

Turning them off is a reminder that the hard part isn’t model accuracy but governance: GDPR purpose limitation and the EU AI Act leave very narrow exceptions for real-time biometric identification. If such systems ever return, a privacy-by-design, multi-agent (agentic) approach could help—distributed agents separately handle legal-basis verification, watchlist minimization, on-device/ephemeral matching, and parallel policy checks before any alert is surfaced. It won’t resolve the ethics debate, but it makes function creep harder and creates auditable trails for DPAs and courts.
We're preferring ignorance to knowledge here because we don't trust the government. Which is weird.

and who would verify that the system had not been modified and is being used in accordance with the law? pinky promise is not enough.police forces in most (EU but not only) countries had proven multiple times that they think the law does not apply to them. they will always abuse such systems.
